steps.title: Find subject and verb
steps.content: The subject is "John Donne’s ___" (the blank holds the noun). The main verb comes much later: "had... been essentially gathering dust." Between them sits a nonrestrictive modifier: "much admired during his lifetime (1572–1631) and in the decades that followed."

steps.title: Punctuate the modifier
steps.content: A nonrestrictive modifier dropped between the subject and its verb must be set off with a comma on each side. The closing comma after "followed" is already there, so the blank needs the opening comma right after the subject noun — and no extra verb, because "had... been gathering" is already the main verb.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'works were': inserts a second main verb "were," leaving the real verb "had... been gathering" without a subject and producing two finite verbs for one subject. ✗
  • Choice B — 'works, were': same problem — an extra finite verb collides with "had... been gathering." ✗
  • Choice C — 'works,': gives the subject "works" plus the opening comma the nonrestrictive modifier needs, letting "had... been gathering" serve as the lone main verb. ✓
  • Choice D — 'works had been': doubles up with the later "had... been gathering," creating a redundant verb string and omitting the required opening comma. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
